title: FLS Prep Supplement Test: Air embolism

questions.text: Which of the following is the most appropriate emergency position for a patient with suspected gas embolism?

answerOptions.text: A) Supine
B) Reverse Trendelenburg
C) Left lateral decubitus with head down
D) Right lateral decubitus with head down

questions.explanation: This position helps trap gas in the right atrium and reduces the risk of embolus entering the pulmonary circulation.
questions.submodule: Fundamentals of Laparoscopic Surgery -  Module 7: Physiology of Pneumoperitoneum

questions.text: Which of the following is least likely to be a clinical sign of gas embolism?

answerOptions.text: A) Hypotension
B) Mill wheel murmur
C) Jugular venous distention
D) Bradycardia

questions.explanation: Gas embolism typically causes tachycardia and hypotension due to obstructed venous return, not bradycardia.
questions.submodule: Fundamentals of Laparoscopic Surgery -  Module 7: Physiology of Pneumoperitoneum

questions.text: If a patient becomes hypotensive during laparoscopy, what is the first recommended action?

answerOptions.text: A) Administer epinephrine
B) Increase insufflation pressure
C) Evacuate the pneumoperitoneum
D) Begin chest compressions

questions.explanation: In the event of hypotension, stop insufflation and allow gas to escape. Then assess ventilatory status, volume status, and rule out bleeding or embolism.
questions.submodule: Fundamentals of Laparoscopic Surgery -  Module 7: Physiology of Pneumoperitoneum

questions.text: Which of the following physiologic changes during a gas embolism is most directly responsible for cardiovascular collapse?

answerOptions.text: A) Increased systemic vascular resistance
B) Decreased venous return to the heart
C) Bradycardia from vagal stimulation
D) Ventricular fibrillation from hypercapnia

questions.explanation: Gas embolism causes obstruction of right heart inflow, resulting in a sudden drop in preload and severe hypotension, which is the main mechanism behind cardiovascular collapse.
questions.submodule: Fundamentals of Laparoscopic Surgery -  Module 7: Physiology of Pneumoperitoneum

questions.text: Which of the following best explains the "mill wheel" murmur heard during a significant gas embolism?

answerOptions.text: A) Rapid collapse of the left atrium
B) Gas bubbles mixing with blood in the right heart
C) Turbulent flow across a stenotic pulmonary valve
D) Fluid-air interface in the pulmonary capillaries

questions.explanation: The mill wheel murmur is caused by the churning of blood and gas in the right atrium or ventricle, creating a characteristic splashing auscultatory sound.
questions.submodule: Fundamentals of Laparoscopic Surgery -  Module 7: Physiology of Pneumoperitoneum

questions.text: During laparoscopic insufflation, which of the following factors increases the risk of clinically significant gas embolism?

answerOptions.text: A) Use of warmed insufflation gas
B) Insufflation with helium rather than carbon dioxide
C) Operating in Trendelenburg position
D) Low intra-abdominal pressure

questions.explanation: Helium is poorly soluble in blood and persists longer, increasing the risk and severity of embolism compared to CO₂, which is rapidly absorbed.
questions.submodule: Fundamentals of Laparoscopic Surgery -  Module 7: Physiology of Pneumoperitoneum

questions.text: What is the most definitive treatment for a massive CO₂ gas embolism during laparoscopy when initial measures fail?

answerOptions.text: A) Increase insufflation pressure and continue the procedure
B) Administer 100% oxygen and place patient in lithotomy position
C) Insert central venous catheter to aspirate gas from right atrium
D) Perform defibrillation and resume insufflation

questions.explanation: Aspiration via central venous catheter is a key advanced intervention to remove gas and restore cardiac function after positioning and pneumoperitoneum evacuation.
questions.submodule: Fundamentals of Laparoscopic Surgery -  Module 7: Physiology of Pneumoperitoneum

questions.text: Why is CO₂ embolism typically less severe than embolism from inert gases like helium?

answerOptions.text: A) CO₂ is less likely to reach the right atrium
B) CO₂ dissolves rapidly in blood and is exhaled
C) CO₂ is filtered out by the liver
D) CO₂ embolism cannot cause hemodynamic compromise

questions.explanation: CO₂'s high solubility allows it to be absorbed and eliminated more easily, making CO₂ embolisms often self-limited compared to those caused by insoluble gases.
questions.submodule: Fundamentals of Laparoscopic Surgery -  Module 7: Physiology of Pneumoperitoneum
